file.content:
-- $Id: testes/locals.lua $
-- See Copyright Notice in file all.lua

print('testing local variables and environments')

local debug = require"debug"

local tracegc = require"tracegc"


-- bug in 5.1:

local function f(x) x = nil; return x end
assert(f(10) == nil)

local function f() local x; return x end
assert(f(10) == nil)

local function f(x) x = nil; local y; return x, y end
assert(f(10) == nil and select(2, f(20)) == nil)

do
  local i = 10
  do local i = 100; assert(i==100) end
  do local i = 1000; assert(i==1000) end
  assert(i == 10)
  if i ~= 10 then
    local i = 20
  else
    local i = 30
    assert(i == 30)
  end
end



f = nil

local f
local x = 1

a = nil
load('local a = {}')()
assert(a == nil)

function f (a)
  local _1, _2, _3, _4, _5
  local _6, _7, _8, _9, _10
  local x = 3
  local b = a
  local c,d = a,b
  if (d == b) then
    local x = 'q'
    x = b
    assert(x == 2)
  else
    assert(nil)
  end
  assert(x == 3)
  local f = 10
end

local b=10
local a; repeat local b; a,b=1,2; assert(a+1==b); until a+b==3


assert(x == 1)

f(2)
assert(type(f) == 'function')


local function getenv (f)
  local a,b = debug.getupvalue(f, 1)
  assert(a == '_ENV')
  return b
end

-- test for global table of loaded chunks
assert(getenv(load"a=3") == _G)
local c = {}; local f = load("a = 3", nil, nil, c)
assert(getenv(f) == c)
assert(c.a == nil)
f()
assert(c.a == 3)

-- old test for limits for special instructions
do
  local i = 2
  local p = 4    -- p == 2^i
  repeat
    for j=-3,3 do
      assert(load(string.format([[local a=%s;
                                        a=a+%s;
                                        assert(a ==2^%s)]], j, p-j, i), '')) ()
      assert(load(string.format([[local a=%s;
                                        a=a-%s;
                                        assert(a==-2^%s)]], -j, p-j, i), '')) ()
      assert(load(string.format([[local a,b=0,%s;
                                        a=b-%s;

file.content:
print "to-be-closed variables in coroutines"

do
  -- yielding inside closing metamethods

  local trace = {}
  local co = coroutine.wrap(function ()

    trace[#trace + 1] = "nowX"

    -- will be closed after 'y'
    local x <close> = func2close(function (_, msg)
      assert(msg == nil)
      trace[#trace + 1] = "x1"
      coroutine.yield("x")
      trace[#trace + 1] = "x2"
    end)

    return pcall(function ()
      do   -- 'z' will be closed first
        local z <close> = func2close(function (_, msg)
          assert(msg == nil)
          trace[#trace + 1] = "z1"
          coroutine.yield("z")
          trace[#trace + 1] = "z2"
        end)
      end

      trace[#trace + 1] = "nowY"

      -- will be closed after 'z'
      local y <close> = func2close(function(_, msg)
        assert(msg == nil)
        trace[#trace + 1] = "y1"
        coroutine.yield("y")
        trace[#trace + 1] = "y2"
      end)

      return 10, 20, 30
    end)
  end)

  assert(co() == "z")
  assert(co() == "y")
  assert(co() == "x")
  checktable({co()}, {true, 10, 20, 30})
  checktable(trace, {"nowX", "z1", "z2", "nowY", "y1", "y2", "x1", "x2"})

end


do
  -- yielding inside closing metamethods while returning
  -- (bug in 5.4.3)

  local extrares    -- result from extra yield (if any)

  local function check (body, extra, ...)
    local t = table.pack(...)   -- expected returns
    local co = coroutine.wrap(body)
    if extra then
      extrares = co()    -- runs until first (extra) yield
    end
    local res = table.pack(co())   -- runs until yield inside '__close'
    assert(res.n == 2 and res[2] == nil)
    local res2 = table.pack(co())   -- runs until end of function
    assert(res2.n == t.n)
    for i = 1, #t do
      if t[i] == "x" then
        assert(res2[i] == res[1])    -- value that was closed
      else
        assert(res2[i] == t[i])
      end
    end
  end

  local function foo ()
    local x <close> = func2close(coroutine.yield)
    local extra <close> = func2close(function (self)
      assert(self == extrares)

content.content:
1	//! Auxiliary library: helper functions for building Lua libraries.
2	//!
3	//! C source: `reference/lua-5.4.7/src/lauxlib.c` (1127 lines, ~50 functions)
4	//! Target crate: `lua-stdlib`
5	//!
6	//! This module provides the high-level `luaL_*` API layer that sits on top of
7	//! the raw `lua_*` C API. In Rust we translate each `luaL_*` function as a
8	//! free function receiving `&mut LuaState` rather than a method, matching the
9	//! structure of the other stdlib modules.
10	//!
11	//! PORT NOTE: The C buffer system (`luaL_Buffer`) uses a small inline initial
12	//! buffer backed by a Lua-stack userdata box on overflow. In Rust we replace
13	//! this with a plain `Vec<u8>` (`LuaBuffer`), dropping all the C-internal
14	//! `UBox` / `resizebox` / `boxgc` / `boxmt` / `newbox` / `buffonstack`
15	//! machinery. The public interface remains compatible.
16	//!
17	//! PORT NOTE: File-loading functions (`load_filex`) reference `std::fs` which
18	//! is banned outside `lua-cli`. Those functions carry `TODO(port)` markers.
19	
20	// TODO(port): LuaState, LuaValue, LuaError, GcRef, LuaString, LuaUserData,
21	// LuaDebug, and LuaType are defined across lua-vm / lua-types. Imports will be
22	// wired in Phase B. Using local stubs for Phase A so rustc can parse the file.
23	
24	use lua_types::{
25	    error::LuaError,
26	    value::LuaValue,
27	    gc::GcRef,
28	    string::LuaString,
29	    userdata::LuaUserData,
30	    LuaType,
31	    LuaStatus,
32	    arith::ArithOp,
33	};
34	use crate::state_stub::{LuaState, LuaStateStubExt as _, lua_CFunction, upvalue_index, CompareOp, LuaDebug};
35	
36	// ── Constants ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
37	
38	/// Number of stack frames to show in the first part of a traceback.
39	/// C: `#define LEVELS1 10`
40	const LEVELS1: i32 = 10;
41	
42	/// Number of stack frames to show in the second part of a traceback.
43	/// C: `#define LEVELS2 11`
44	const LEVELS2: i32 = 11;
45	
46	/// Index (1-based) in the reference table that heads the free-list of recycled
47	/// references. Placed after the last predefined registry key.
48	/// C: `#define freelist (LUA_RIDX_LAST + 1)` where `LUA_RIDX_LAST = 2`.
49	const FREELIST_REF: i64 = 3; // LUA_RIDX_GLOBALS (2) + 1
50	
51	/// Pseudo-reference returned by `lua_ref` when the pushed value was `nil`.
52	/// C: `#define LUA_REFNIL (-1)`
53	pub const LUA_REFNIL: i32 = -1;
54	
55	/// Pseudo-reference meaning "no reference" (never created by `lua_ref`).
56	/// C: `#define LUA_NOREF (-2)`
57	pub const LUA_NOREF: i32 = -2;
58	
59	/// Extended error code: file-related I/O error from `load_filex`.
60	/// C: `#define LUA_ERRFILE (LUA_ERRERR + 1)` = 6.
61	pub const LUA_ERRFILE: i32 = 6;
62	
63	/// Registry key for the table of loaded modules.
64	/// C: `#define LUA_LOADED_TABLE "_LOADED"`
65	pub const LUA_LOADED_TABLE: &[u8] = b"_LOADED";
66	
67	/// Registry key for the table of preloaded loaders.
68	/// C: `#define LUA_PRELOAD_TABLE "_PRELOAD"`
69	pub const LUA_PRELOAD_TABLE: &[u8] = b"_PRELOAD";
70	
71	/// Name of the global environment table.
72	/// C: `#define LUA_GNAME "_G"`
73	pub const LUA_GNAME: &[u8] = b"_G";
74	
75	/// Metatable name / file-handle key for the IO library.
76	/// C: `#define LUA_FILEHANDLE "FILE*"`
77	pub const LUA_FILE_HANDLE: &[u8] = b"FILE*";
78	
79	/// Pseudo-index for the Lua registry.
80	/// C: `#define LUA_REGISTRYINDEX (-LUAI_MAXSTACK - 1000)`

delta.thinking: The
error message says "pcall_k failed: Runtime: attempt to call a nil value" -
but the error comes from a pcall in Lua (the `pcall`
function call inside the coroutine at line 829). The error is happening during "
to-be-closed variables in coroutines".
Let me look at the pcall_k implementation and the coroutine.wrap implementation to understand what's going wrong.
Also, this is a pcall call in Lua code, so I need to look at

how our VM handles the pcall opcode.

Actually
, let me think about what exactly is happening. The error is:
The
actual error is "attempt to call a nil value" - the "pcall_k failed" part is just the wrapper in main.rs
. So something in the test is trying to call a nil value.
The test uses coroutine.wrap, to-be-closed variables with `<close>`,
and pcall inside the coroutine, so one of those features isn't working correctly and
is leaving something nil when it shouldn't be.
The
error is happening because something that should be callable is nil. Looking at the test output, it
successfully prints the "to-be-closed variables in coroutines" message, which means execution reaches that
point. The issue appears when the test tries to create a coroutine with `coroutine.wrap`
and then use it—either the wrap function itself isn't working properly
, or something inside the coroutine is nil when it shouldn't be. The PORTING.md mentions coroutines
are stubbed, but the test is clearly trying to use them, so I need to check whether
the earlier coroutine tests actually ran with real coroutines or if they were skipped somehow.

file.content:
print"testing to-be-closed variables"

local function stack(n) n = ((n == 0) or stack(n - 1)) end

local function func2close (f, x, y)
  local obj = setmetatable({}, {__close = f})
  if x then
    return x, obj, y
  else
    return obj
  end
end


do
  local a = {}
  do
    local b <close> = false   -- not to be closed
    local x <close> = setmetatable({"x"}, {__close = function (self)
                                                   a[#a + 1] = self[1] end})

. inside the `__close` metamethods
4. When `
aux_wrap` runs the wrapped function, it executes the `pcall` which creates variables

apping the code that contains the yields, so the yields happen inside the protected call

content.thinking:
So `coroutine.wrap` returns an `aux_wrap` function. The `aux_wrap` implementation runs the wrapped function synchronously (not as a real coroutine), captures yield calls into a buffer, and then dispenses the results one batch per call.

The error is "attempt to call a nil value". Let me think about what's happening:

1. The test creates a `coroutine.wrap` function that wraps a function
2. That function uses `pcall(function() ... end)` inside it
3. The wrapped function body calls `coroutine.yield("z")`, `coroutine.yield("y")`, etc. inside the `__close` metamethods


4. When `aux_wrap` runs the wrapped function, it executes the `pcall` which creates variables with `__close` metamethods
5. As those variables go out of scope, their `__close` metamethods trigger and call `coroutine.yield`, which buffers the values instead of actually yielding

The issue is that `pcall` is wrapping the code that contains the yields, so the yields happen inside the protected call context. the return of the outer function, then `x` gets closed and yields "x" to the buffer. So the sequence should be ["z"], ["y"], ["x"], followed by the final return [true, 10, 20, 30], and each `co()` call retrieves them in order. But the error about calling a nil value is happening during execution, so I need to check what `pcall` actually does in this implementation and whether it's properly handling the wrapped function.

Looking at the error message more carefully, `pcall_k failed` is coming from the Rust side, not from Lua itself, which suggests the issue might be in how `pcall` is being invoked or how the environment is set up when the wrapped function tries to call it. Looking at the test execution flow, when `co()` is called it runs the wrapped function which creates a close variable and then calls `pcall` to execute some code inside it. The issue is that `pcall` needs to be available in the function's environment, but it seems like it might not be properly accessible when the coroutine executes, even though earlier tests with `pcall` in `__close` handlers worked fine. I'm tracing through how `pcall` interacts with the `__close` metamethods—when the function returns, the to-be-closed variables get closed through `luaD_closeprotected` after the function body executes but before `pcall` itself returns, and I need to understand how our VM handles this mechanism.

I need to understand the issue more deeply. The error is "attempt to call a nil value" happening during "to-be-closed variables in coroutines". Let me look at the `aux_wrap` implementation again.

The issue with `aux_wrap`:
1. It runs the wrapped function synchronously
2. The wrapped function calls `pcall(function() ... end)` 
3. Inside the pcall'd function, to-be-closed variables are used
4. When the inner function returns, `__close` is called, which calls `coroutine.yield`
5. `coroutine.yield` with a yield buffer captures the yield and returns 0


6. When `pcall_fn` is invoked inside the wrapped function, it triggers `protected_call`, which then executes the lambda containing the to-be-closed variable
7. As that variable gets cleaned up, `coroutine.yield` is invoked, but the yield buffer intercepts it and returns 0 arguments instead of letting the yield propagate normally to the `__close` metamethod

The critical issue is what happens when `coroutine.yield` returns to the `__close` caller—the yield buffer's interception breaks the expected control flow.

Actually, I'm realizing there's a deeper problem here. When `aux_wrap` runs the function synchronously via `state.call(nargs, -1)`, it executes the outer function which contains a `pcall` wrapping an inner function with a to-be-closed variable that yields. The structure creates a nested control flow where the yield happens during cleanup, which the yield buffer wasn't designed to handle. I need to trace through the execution order more carefully. The `z` variable is scoped to the `do` block, so when that block exits, `z.__close` gets called and yields "z". Then `y` is created in the outer function scope, and when the function returns its values, `y.__close` is called and yields "y" before the pcall completes. So the pcall should return true along with the three values, which then get passed back to the outer function.

But before those results are returned, `x.__close` is called in the outer function, yielding "x" and returning 0 values. The final wrapped function result should be the pcall's return values: true, 10, 20, 30. All the close handlers should be captured in order: "z", "y", "x", then the final result.

The actual error is "attempt to call a nil value", which suggests something's wrong with how the function is being invoked in the Rust wrapper. Let me check the `aux_wrap` implementation to see if the function index is being retrieved and called correctly.

I'm noticing that `push_yield_buffer()` is called after the function and arguments are already on the stack—if that function pushes something onto the stack itself, it would throw off the argument count or the function's position, causing the call to fail.

content.thinking:
Good. So `push_yield_buffer()` just pushes a new empty `Vec<Vec<LuaValue>>` onto the `yield_buffers` Vec - it doesn't affect the Lua stack. So `push_yield_buffer` doesn't affect stack positions.

Now let me look at `state.call` - I need to see how `state.call(nargs, -1)` works and what it does with the stack.

Actually, the key issue is in `aux_wrap`:
```rust
let nargs = state.get_top();
let func = state.value_at(upvalue_index(1));


```

This is how `aux_wrap` handles args from the outer call - the args passed to `co(args...)` get forwarded to the wrapped function. For the first call `co()` with no args, `nargs` would be 0, then the function gets pushed onto the stack and positioned correctly before the call.

On the second call, the batch index increments and `maybe_next` returns the next batch value. The tricky part is understanding what happens when `pcall` runs inside - it calls `state.protected_call` which executes the inner function, and when `co_yield` is invoked within that protected context, it captures into the yield buffer and returns 0. This should work correctly unless there's an issue with how the insert operation handles the state.

Let me trace through `aux_wrap` more carefully. When `state.get_top()` is called, it returns the argument count on the stack at entry. For a call like `co()` with no arguments, that's 0, but for `co("x")` with one argument, it would be 1. The function then pushes the wrapped function and conditionally inserts it before the arguments if any exist.

The real issue might be with how the yield buffer and protected call interact. When `pcall` is invoked inside the wrapped coroutine function, the VM needs to resolve `pcall` from the global environment. If there's something wrong with how `_ENV` is set up during coroutine execution, or if the protected call mechanism is interfering with the environment lookup, `pcall` could resolve to nil instead of the actual function. The test output shows it's passing the earlier to-be-closed variable tests but crashing specifically in the coroutine section, so the issue is likely in how `aux_wrap` handles the wrapped function or how it interacts with the to-be-closed mechanism. Let me trace through what `aux_wrap` is doing with the function pointer and state management. I need to understand what `upvalue_index` does—it should give me the index for accessing the first upvalue of the current closure, so let me look up its definition.

35	/// Bare function callable from Lua. C: `lua_CFunction`.
36	#[allow(non_camel_case_types)]
37	pub type lua_CFunction = fn(&mut LuaState) -> Result<usize, LuaError>;
38	
39	/// Pseudo-index for the `i`-th upvalue of a C function.
40	/// C: `#define lua_upvalueindex(i)`
41	pub fn upvalue_index(i: i32) -> i32 {
42	    -1_001_000 - i
43	}
44	
45	/// Comparison operations (eq, lt, le). C: `LUA_OP*`.
46	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
47	pub enum CompareOp {
48	    Eq,
49	    Lt,
50	    Le,
51	}
52	
53	/// Reader-callback type for `lua_load`. C: `lua_Reader`.
54	pub type LuaReader<'a> = dyn FnMut() -> Option<Vec<u8>> + 'a;
55	
56	/// Writer-callback type for `lua_dump`. C: `lua_Writer`.
57	pub type LuaWriter<'a> = dyn FnMut(&[u8]) -> Result<(), LuaError> + 'a;
58	
59	/// Debug introspection record. C: `lua_Debug`.
60	#[derive(Debug, Default, Clone)]
61	pub struct LuaDebug {
62	    pub name: Option<Vec<u8>>,
63	    pub namewhat: Vec<u8>,
64	    pub what: u8,
65	    pub source: Vec<u8>,
66	    pub short_src: Vec<u8>,
67	    pub linedefined: i32,
68	    pub lastlinedefined: i32,
69	    pub currentline: i32,
70	    pub nups: u8,
71	    pub nparams: u8,
72	    pub isvararg: bool,
73	    pub istailcall: bool,
74	    pub ftransfer: u16,
75	    pub ntransfer: u16,
76	    /// Active CallInfo index, set by `get_stack`/`get_stack_level` and read by
77	    /// `get_info`/`get_local_at`/`set_local_at`. Mirrors C's `lua_Debug.i_ci`
78	    /// (a raw pointer in C; an index here).
79	    pub(crate) i_ci_idx: Option<CallInfoIdx>,
80	}
81	
82	impl LuaDebug {
83	    pub fn name_bytes(&self) -> &[u8] { self.name.as_deref().unwrap_or(b"?") }
84	    pub fn namewhat_bytes(&self) -> &[u8] { &self.namewhat }
85	    pub fn what_bytes(&self) -> &[u8] { match self.what { b'L' => b"Lua", b'C' => b"C", b'm' => b"main", _ => b"?" } }
86	    pub fn short_src_bytes(&self) -> &[u8] { &self.short_src }

I see. So `upvalue_index(1)` returns `-1_001_001`. 

Now looking at `is_upvalue(idx)`:
- `LUA_REGISTRYINDEX = -1_001_000`
- `is_upvalue(idx) = idx < LUA_REGISTRYINDEX = idx < -1_001_000`
- `upvalue_index(1) = -1_001_001` which is indeed `< -1_001_000`

So `-1_001_001 < -1_001_000` is TRUE. So `is_upvalue(-1_001_001)` is TRUE. Good.


In `index_to_value`, the calculation shows that `upval_n = 1`, which maps to `ccl.upvalues[0]` — the wrapped function. So retrieving the value at `upvalue_index(1)` should give me the wrapped function. But I'm getting a "nil value" error, which means something's going wrong. Let me trace through `aux_wrap` to see where the wrapped function is actually being stored.

Examining the code that retrieves the C closure's identity and then looks up its state in the global wrap iterator cache...

Now I'm setting up the first call to the wrapped function by preparing the yield buffer and arranging the arguments on the stack. When `aux_wrap` is called with no arguments, the stack starts empty, `nargs` becomes 0, and I push the wrapped function onto it. Since there are no arguments to reposition, I call the function directly with 0 arguments. The `call_k` function expects the function to be at position `top - nargs - 1`, which in this case is the top of the stack. But I'm getting a "nil value" error, which suggests the function isn't where `call_k` expects it to be—let me trace through how `call_k` actually locates and invokes the function. The issue is that when `pcall` wraps the function, it's setting up the call stack in a way that breaks the yield mechanism. The `pcall` implementation is pushing an extra boolean and adjusting the argument count, which means when the wrapped function tries to yield through `__close`, the yield buffer gets corrupted or the return value doesn't propagate correctly back through the `pcall` boundary. Following the protected call, the anonymous function executes and when the `do` block exits, `z.__close` triggers a yield that gets captured. Then `y` is created, and when the pcall'd function returns its values, `y.__close` also yields and gets captured. The pcall returns successfully with those values, and as the outer function prepares to return, `x.__close` fires one final yield that's captured before everything unwinds.

So the sequence of yields is ["z"], ["y"], ["x"] from the three close handlers, and the final return value is [true, 10, 20, 30] from the pcall result.

When `aux_wrap` collects these, it builds batches as [["z"], ["y"], ["x"], [true, 10, 20, 30]]. Then it removes the first batch ["z"] and stores the remaining batches [["y"], ["x"], [true, 10, 20, 30]] in `wrap_iter_state` for subsequent iterations.

Now I'm tracing through the subsequent calls to `co()` — each one retrieves the next batch from storage and yields it, so the second call returns "y", the third returns "x", and the fourth should return [true, 10, 20, 30]. I need to trace through how the iterator state is being stored and mutated. When `wrap_iter_state` inserts the batches with index 0, subsequent calls use `get_mut` to modify that index in-place, so each call increments the stored index and returns the corresponding batch until we've exhausted all of them. Tracing through the test cases to verify the logic: the first three calls return "z", "y", and "x" as expected, and the fourth call should yield the batch [true, 10, 20, 30] which matches what the test expects when unpacked into the table.

The immediate failure with "attempt to call a nil value" right after the test name prints suggests the error occurs in the first `do` block, likely when the coroutine wrapper tries to invoke `call_k` on the Lua state—something in that call chain is receiving a nil value instead of a callable function.
